content.full_text:
Marialee Neighbours 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Alliance of American Insurers 
220 Sansome Street, Ste. 1320 
San Francisco, CA 
94104 
Dear Marialee: 
Administration 
322.5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
February 15, 1985 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Re: 
Our File No. A-85-021 
Thank you for your letter of J~nuary 24, 1985, requesting 
advice with respect to the lobbying disclosure provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (Government Code Sections 81000-91014). 
According to your letter, Professional Women Advocates, an 
organization of registered lobbyists, is hosting a reception for 
legislators on February 26. 
Each member of the organization will 
pay $l5 toward the cost of the reception, although it is antici-
pated that only a portion of each member's payment will be used 
in connection with the event. 
You asked bow the members of your 
organization should report the reception on their quarterly 
Lobbyist Reports (Form 620). 
Each lobbyist must report the reception in the "Activity 
Expenses" portion of the Form 620 (Part III, Section A.2.), and 
must provide the name and official position of each reportable 
person who is a guest at the event and the total amount of benefit 
provided to each. 
To determine the amount of benefit provided, 
you should divide the entire cost of the event by the total number 
of people who attended. 
For example, if the cost of the event is 
$300 and a total of 50 people attend, including the hosting 
lobbyists, the pro rata cost per person will be $6. 
Each lobbyist 
must report the entire $6 as the amount benefiting each official. 
In the "Total Amount of Activity" column, each lobbyist will 
report that portion of her $15 payment which was actually used 
to pay for the reception, and as a footnote will indicate the 
entire cost of the event, the number of people who attended, 
and that her payment represents a portion of the total cost of 
the event. 
Assuming it was not used to benefit officials, the 
portion of the $15 payment which was not used to pay for the 


Marialee Neighbours 
February 15, 1985 
Page 2 
reception will be reported as a "General Lobbying Expens.e." 
Finally, the lobbyists must also retain in their records a list 
of the non-reportable persons who attend as guests. 
You also asked about the $10 gift prohibition. 
Government 
Code Section 86203 prohibits lobbyists from making or arranging 
for the making of gifts to one legislative or administrative 
official aggregating $10 in a calendar month. 
Therefore, the 
pro rata benefit to each individual legislator should not exceed 
$10. 
In addition, each lobbyist will need to aggregate the 
benefit to each legislator attending the event with any other 
gifts that lobbyist has made to that legislator during the 
calendar~month. 
I hope this answers your questions. 
Please let me know 
if you need any further information • .. 
Sincerely, 
Carla J. Wardlow 
Political Reform Consultant 


James S. Stickles 
Regional Vice President 
January 24, 1985 
Ms. Carla Wardlow 
FAIR Political Practices Commission 
p. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Dear Carla: 
Alliance of American Insurers 
Pacific Region 
220 Sansome Street, Suite 1320 
San Francisco, California 94104 
415-362·0870 
This letter will confirm my telephone conversation with you regarding lobbyist reporting 
for the Professional Women Advocates' reception on February 26. 
As I indicated to you, 
we will be hosting a reception for California legislators and will be collecting $15 00 
from our members. 
However, only a portion of that $15.00 will go for cost of the 
reception which will be less than $10.00 per person. 
Could you please send me a letter indicating how our lobbyist members should report this 
$15.00 
contribution to the Professional Women Advocates when completing their lobbying 
reports. 
Additionally, I would appreciate it if you could restate your comment that 
each lobbyist must be cautioned not to spend more than $10.00 per legislator in the 
entire month of February. 
As I recall, you indicated that, if a lobbyist had, for 
example, spent $3.00 on a legislator for the month of February, then that lobbyist 
could only spend another $7.00 on that same legislator. 
Thank you so much for your help. 
Best regards. 
Sincerely, 
Marialee Neighbours 
Assistant Vice President and Counsel 
MN:vcy

sections.question: According to your letter, Professional Women Advocates, an organization of registered lobbyists, is hosting a reception for legislators on February 26. Each member of the organization will pay $15 toward the cost of the reception, although it is anticipated that only a portion of each member's payment will be used in connection with the event. You asked how the members of your organization should report the reception on their quarterly Lobbyist Reports (Form 620). You also asked about the $10 gift prohibition.
sections.conclusion: Each lobbyist must report the reception in the "Activity Expenses" portion of the Form 620 (Part III, Section A.2.), and must provide the name and official position of each reportable person who is a guest at the event and the total amount of benefit provided to each. To determine the amount of benefit provided, you should divide the entire cost of the event by the total number of people who attended. In the "Total Amount of Activity" column, each lobbyist will report that portion of her $15 payment which was actually used to pay for the reception, and as a footnote will indicate the entire cost of the event, the number of people who attended, and that her payment represents a portion of the total cost of the event. The pro rata benefit to each individual legislator should not exceed $10, and each lobbyist must aggregate the benefit to each legislator attending the event with any other gifts made to that legislator during the calendar month.
sections.question_synthetic: How should registered lobbyist members of Professional Women Advocates report their $15 contributions to a legislative reception on their Form 620 Lobbyist Reports, and what are the implications of the $10 gift prohibition?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Lobbyists must report the pro-rata cost of the reception as activity expenses on Form 620, allocating the total event cost divided by attendees to each legislator. Each lobbyist's pro-rata benefit to any individual legislator cannot exceed $10 and must be aggregated with other gifts to that legislator in the same calendar month.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (January 24, 1985) and FPPC response letter (February 15, 1985). Analysis focuses on the FPPC advice letter as the primary document. Clear question and conclusion sections present in the response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: According to your letter, Professional Women Advocates, an organization of registered lobbyists, is hosting a reception for legislators on February 26. Each member of the organization will pay $15 toward the cost of the reception, although it is anticipated that only a portion of each member's payment will be used in connection with the event. You asked how the members of your organization should report the reception on their quarterly Lobbyist Reports (Form 620). You also asked about the $10 gift prohibition.

CONCLUSION: Each lobbyist must report the reception in the "Activity Expenses" portion of the Form 620 (Part III, Section A.2.), and must provide the name and official position of each reportable person who is a guest at the event and the total amount of benefit provided to each. To determine the amount of benefit provided, you should divide the entire cost of the event by the total number of people who attended. In the "Total Amount of Activity" column, each lobbyist will report that portion of her $15 payment which was actually used to pay for the reception, and as a footnote will indicate the entire cost of the event, the number of people who attended, and that her payment represents a portion of the total cost of the event. The pro rata benefit to each individual legislator should not exceed $10, and each lobbyist must aggregate the benefit to each legislator attending the event with any other gifts made to that legislator during the calendar month.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter to Alliance of American Insurers regarding proper reporting of a Professional Women Advocates reception for California legislators. The letter provides detailed guidance on Form 620 filing requirements and compliance with the $10 gift prohibition under Government Code Section 86203.
